hook: Turkish-Cypriot bakery, butcher and market anchoring the Green Lanes ladder since 1981
facts: Founded 1981 by Yasar Halim at 491-493 Green Lanes
Bakes bread, baklava and borek on-site; butcher and greengrocer attached
Halim arrived from Cyprus aged 18 in 1962, learning the trade in Kentish Town
Still family-run after his death in 2020
w: Yasar Halim came through Victoria station from Cyprus at 18 and learned his trade over a Kentish Town oven before opening here in 1981, anchoring the Green Lanes ladder for the Turkish-Cypriot diaspora. Bread, baklava and borek baked at the back, butcher and greengrocer up front. He died in 2020; the family bakes on.

hook: Cobbled-courtyard bakery off Columbia Road from London's self-taught 'rock 'n' roll baker'
facts: Lily Jones started selling cakes off Brick Lane when the 2008 recession hit
Bakery opened on Ezra Street in a brick courtyard in 2010
Known early for a notorious anatomical 'brain cake'
Cafe trades busiest on Columbia Road flower-market Sundays
w: Lily Jones, a philosophy graduate turned graphic designer, sold her first cakes off Brick Lane when the recession bit, earning the tag 'London's rock 'n' roll baker' for things like a notorious anatomical brain cake. Since 2010 she bakes in a tucked-away brick courtyard off Ezra Street. Sundays, the flower-market queue snakes out.

hook: London's self-styled oldest organic bakery, born from a squat and a bathtub mixing bowl
facts: Karl Heinz Rossbach began baking sourdough here in 1982
Started while squatting a former post office, mixing dough in an old bathtub
Claims to be London's oldest organic bakery
Still at 76 Landor Road, selling eccles cakes, bloomers and pain au levain
w: Karl Heinz Rossbach started baking sourdough here in 1982 while squatting a disused post office, mixing his dough, they say, in an old cast-iron bathtub. The counterculture-meets-old-Europe ethos stuck: London's self-styled oldest organic bakery still turns out eccles cakes, bloomers and pain au levain on Landor Road.

hook: Michelin-trained chef's artisan bakery feeding Greenwich Market off stone-based ovens
facts: Founded 2003 by former Michelin-starred chef Paul Rhodes
Rhodes trained under Pierre Koffmann at La Tante Claire
Shop sits on King William Walk by Greenwich Market
Now one of London's larger independent wholesale bakers
w: Paul Rhodes earned a Michelin star and a stint under Pierre Koffmann before swapping the pass for the proving room in 2003, baking on stone-based ovens by Greenwich Market. Croissants laminated properly, sourdough with a crust that argues back. The chef who decided bread made the harder craft.

hook: Hole-in-the-wall Armenian flatbread bakery on Ridley Road, topped to order
facts: Tiny bakery at 132 Ridley Road, on the Dalston market strip
Specialises in Armenian-style lavash and flatbreads baked to order
Named for Mount Ararat, the Armenian heartland's lost peak
Breads sold plain or topped with meat, cheese or egg
w: A hole-in-the-wall on the Ridley Road market strip, baking Armenian-style flatbread to order: dough slapped out, blistered, handed over hot and topped with cheese, egg or spiced meat. Named for Mount Ararat, the snow-capped peak the Armenians lost to a 1932 border. Diaspora baking at its most direct.

hook: Survivor of London's oldest kosher bakery dynasty, founded on an East End barrow in 1888
facts: Grodzinski began in 1888 when Harris and Judith Grodzinski hired kosher ovens in the East End
By the 1960s the largest kosher bakery in Europe, supplying Selfridges and Harrods
Golders Green Road branch carries the family name
Known for challah, bilkele and Chanukah doughnuts
w: Harris and Judith Grodzinski stepped off the boat from a Belarusian shtetl in 1888 and hired kosher ovens in the East End, starting from a barrow. By the 1960s the largest kosher bakery in Europe, supplying Selfridges and Harrods. The name survives on Golders Green Road: challah, bilkele, doughnuts at Chanukah.

hook: Near-round-the-clock kosher bakery whose rugelach and bagels feed northwest London
facts: Kosher bakery on Golders Green Road since 1987
Trades through the night on busy weekends
Famous for an enormous range of chocolate, cinnamon and vanilla rugelach
Has catered the Israeli Embassy and the House of Lords
w: Golders Green's late-night engine room since 1987, baking through the small hours so the Sabbath challah and a wall of rugelach, chocolate, cinnamon, vanilla, hit the shelves warm. The salt-beef bagel has its loyalists; the Israeli Embassy and the House of Lords have both put in orders. Sleep is for amateurs.

hook: Run by London's oldest baking family, on a hay-wagon line traced to 1820
facts: Robert Dunn came south by hay wagon to bake in Highgate in 1820
The family claims to be London's oldest baking dynasty
Now run by sixth-generation baker Lewis Freeman
Trades from The Broadway, Crouch End
w: Robert Dunn rode south from Northamptonshire on a hay wagon in 1820 to bake on Highgate Hill, founding what the family reckons London's oldest baking line. Six generations on, Lewis Freeman holds the Crouch End ovens. Two centuries of bread, unbroken, under a clock-tower sky.

hook: Sally Clarke's pioneering Kensington kitchen and bakery, Chez Panisse cooking brought to London
facts: Sally Clarke opened on Kensington Church Street in December 1984
Inspired by Alice Waters and Chez Panisse in Berkeley
Provisions shop and bakery added five years later
Long supplied bread to the River Cafe and others
w: Sally Clarke trained in Paris, fell under Alice Waters at Chez Panisse, then opened on Kensington Church Street days before Christmas 1984, doing seasonal Californian cooking when London barely knew the idea. The bakery next door followed, its loaves quietly feeding the city's best kitchens. Quietly influential, decades deep.

hook: Railway-arch sourdough bakery at Spa Terminus, public only on Saturday mornings
facts: Founded 2010, baking under a Bermondsey railway arch
Sits in the Spa Terminus cluster off Dockley Road
Sells direct to the public from a stall on Saturday mornings only
Wholesales sourdough and pastries across London
w: One of the sourdough arches that turned Bermondsey's Spa Terminus into a Saturday pilgrimage, baking since 2010 under the brick vaults where the trains rumble overhead. Wholesale all week; doors open to the public only on Saturday mornings, when the queue forms for warm loaves and pastries. Blink and you miss the window.

hook: Borough Market doughnut shrine and baking school from St John's former head baker
facts: Opened 2013 at Borough Market by Justin Gellatly
Gellatly spent twelve years as head baker at St John
Famed for custard-filled doughnuts dubbed 'pillows of joy'
Runs a busy adjoining baking school
w: Justin Gellatly spent twelve years building St John's legendary bread before opening here in 2013, and the Borough Market crowds still scrum for his custard-filled doughnuts, the 'pillows of joy'. He baked rolls for a royal wedding; now he teaches the rest of us. Sourdough on one side, a queue for sugar on the other.

hook: Portobello Road shop that introduced Britain to the red velvet cupcake
facts: First opened on Portobello Road in 2004 by Tarek Malouf
Idea sparked over a North Carolina Thanksgiving
Credited with bringing American cupcakes and red velvet to the UK
Recipe books became bestsellers
w: Tarek Malouf spent a Thanksgiving in North Carolina, ate his weight in cupcakes and banana cream pie, and wondered why London had none. The answer opened on Portobello Road in 2004, more or less inventing the British cupcake craze and the red velvet obsession. Whatever you think of the frosting, blame starts here.

hook: Fashion-world chocolatier and artisan bakery hidden on a Connaught Street corner
facts: Opened 2009 by two fashion-industry founders, Joel and Walid
Sits at 12 Connaught Street near Marble Arch
Combines a fine chocolatier with a bakery of dozens of breads and pastries
Known for elaborately decorated cakes
w: Two fashion men, Joel and Walid, bonded over chocolate and proper baking, then in 2009 turned a Connaught Street corner into a small Willy Wonka of a shop near Marble Arch. Seventy-odd breads and pastries, hand-rolled truffles, cakes built like couture. A bakery that wandered in from a different industry and stayed.

hook: Pastel de nata counter anchoring 'Little Portugal' on South Lambeth Road
facts: Stockwell branch of the Gomes family's Lisboa, founded on Golborne Road in 1986
Sits at 147 South Lambeth Road in 'Little Portugal'
Custard tarts baked fresh through the day
Heart of the Vauxhall-Stockwell Portuguese community
w: The Gomes family started Lisboa on Golborne Road in 1986; this Stockwell outpost plants the flag in 'Little Portugal', where families settled between Vauxhall and Brixton from the 1960s. Pastel de nata pulled hot from the oven, scorched on top, eaten standing at the counter with a galao. Lisbon, by way of South Lambeth Road.

hook: Vaulted railway-arch Italian bakery that popularised Rome's cream-filled maritozzi
facts: Opened 2022 by Mitshel Ibrahim of the restaurant Ombra
Set under a vaulted railway arch near Regent's Canal
Helped popularise the Roman maritozzi bun in London
Doubles as a pastificio and deli, making fresh pasta
w: Mitshel Ibrahim, of the restaurant Ombra, opened Forno in 2022 beneath a cathedral-like railway arch near the canal, and did as much as anyone to make London queue for the maritozzi, Rome's split bun stuffed with whipped cream. Focaccia by the slab, fresh pasta at the deli counter. Roman conviction under Hackney brick.
